title: Awards Season Has Gone Cuckoo. Plus: The 10 Best Movies at Sundance.
format: The SAG and Golden Globe nominations get strange (Jared Leto, twice), a GameStop-fueled bubble briefly 'saves' AMC, and Sean, Amanda, and Adam Naiman count down what they liked at a virtual Sundance.

segments.heading: Awards Season Has Gone Cuckoo
segments.note: SAG nominations, the GameStop bubble, and HBO Max's day-and-date bet

films.title: The Little Things
films.note: Jared Leto's second nomination for it — Golden Globe, now SAG. Sean and Amanda can't fathom what in the film merits an award, and wonder whose slot he took (Sound of Metal's Paul Raci among the casualties).

films.title: Hillbilly Elegy
films.note: SAG went for both Glenn Close and Amy Adams. 'This movie was made so that this could happen.'

films.title: Minari
films.note: Overlooked at the Globes, better here — Steven Yeun, Youn Yuh-jung, and a Best Ensemble nod. Reaches theaters the 12th, then VOD.

films.title: Da 5 Bloods
films.note: Two straight snubs for Delroy Lindo; the film landed a Best Ensemble nod. Sean's theory: a summer release fading before awards season.

films.title: Mank
films.note: Led the Globe nominations but missed Best Ensemble here. Amanda Seyfried's omission surprised them; a movie about directors and writers may just not move actors.

films.title: The Father
films.note: Olivia Colman in supporting — good work, but it reads to Amanda like a 'she's the good hang' vote versus the unknowns (Youn Yuh-jung, Helena Zengel).

films.title: Wonder Woman 1984
films.note: Day-and-date on HBO Max; not a critical favorite but it drove the service past 40 million activated users, which is all HBO Max wanted.

films.title: Locked Down
films.note: HBO Max streaming movie with Anne Hathaway and Chiwetel Ejiofor — Doug Liman, Steven Knight. More quality than Sean expects from straight-to-streaming, though the quarantine setting is an asterisk.

films.title: CODA
films.note: Sold to Apple for a record $25 million and swept four of six U.S. Dramatic prizes. The trio finds it the most 'fine,' formulaic Sundance movie imaginable — and Amanda cried anyway. Sian Heder directs.

films.title: Passing
films.note: Rebecca Hall's directorial debut, sold to Netflix for ~$16M. Adapted from Nella Larsen's 1929 novel; Tessa Thompson and Ruth Negga star. Adam: a real filmmaker, sound design and all.

films.title: In the Earth
films.note: Adam's top pick — Ben Wheatley in the woods during a vaguely-defined pandemic. Too long at 110 minutes, but the strobe-and-EDM freakout is the one movie all three wished they'd seen in a theater. Not an Amanda movie.

films.title: In the Same Breath
films.note: Amanda's pick — Nanfu Wang's COVID documentary. The on-the-ground China footage, much of it later censored, is what she can't shake; the American-failure material feels rushed.

films.title: The Sparks Brothers
films.note: Edgar Wright's documentary on the L.A. duo. Way too long and too devoted to its subject — which Sean kind of loves. The brothers stay unknowable even after two-plus hours on camera.

films.title: Judas and the Black Messiah
films.note: Shaka King's docudrama on the FBI and Fred Hampton — a serious grown-up movie sitting oddly in the awards race. All three rate it well above Trial of the Chicago 7 on similar terrain. Sean and Amanda tackle it next week.

films.title: Try Harder!
films.note: Debbie Lum's doc on college-admissions anxiety at a predominantly Asian American high school. One of three vérité high-school films Sean groups together.

films.title: Homeroom
films.note: Peter Nicks's third Oakland film — high school where social activism collides with the students. Sean's kind of movie, alongside Boys State and Minding the Gap.

films.title: Cusp
films.note: Isabel Bethancourt and Parker Hill on three young Texas women in the fever-dream summer after high school.

films.title: All Light Everywhere
films.note: Theo Anthony's essay documentary on body cameras and the weaponization of being watched — the most fully realized thing Sean saw, if not the most commercial. Adam missed it and regrets it.

films.title: A Glitch in the Matrix
films.note: Rodney Ascher on simulation theory — all white guys of a certain age, which Adam reads as the actual subject. Eerie to watch mid-GameStop; Ascher's refusal to supply a counterpoint is the point.

films.title: President
films.note: Camilla Nielsen's doc on Zimbabwe's post-Mugabe election (Chamisa vs. Mnangagwa). Amanda and Sean both watched it like a thriller. Still unsold — the kind of thing a savvy distributor should grab.

films.title: We're All Going to the World's Fair
films.note: Jane Schoenbrun's film about a teenage girl in an online creepypasta game — the only thing Adam forgot he was watching. Specific rather than universal, which is why it feels large.

films.title: Mass
films.note: Fran Kranz's debut — two couples on opposite sides of a school shooting. Stagey but cinematic, with Jason Isaacs, Ann Dowd, Martha Plimpton, and Reed Birney. Sean's under-loved standout.

films.title: Summer of Soul
films.note: Amanda's favorite — Questlove's directorial debut on the 1969 Harlem Cultural Festival. Half unseen concert footage (Stevie Wonder, Nina Simone, Mavis Staples, the Fifth Dimension), half context that enlivens it. A big acquisition feels inevitable.

films: The United States vs. Billie Holiday
Me and Earl and the Dying Girl
Close Encounters of the Third Kind
